content.assessment: This proposal explicitly avoids altering the track structure or selection age, instead layering compensatory funding, staffing, and instructional-time measures onto the existing network. From a network-planning lens, it leaves geographic concentration, capacity caps, and transport arrangements entirely untouched, meaning none of the core network-feasibility questions are addressed by design; the proposal's feasibility is not a matter of network delivery but of funding and staffing allocation within the unchanged network footprint. This is consistent with the lens limitation that network feasibility does not by itself establish educational quality — here the proposal targets quality/equity outcomes while sidestepping network structure altogether.
opportunities: Could be combined with targeted funding specifically for rural/feeder schools experiencing 'dilution', partially offsetting network-level staffing and peer-composition strain without requiring structural change
strengths: Requires no capacity expansion, geographic redistribution, or transport reconfiguration, making it operationally simple to layer onto the existing network without disrupting current provision
Targeting instructional time and teacher-quality gaps in feeder/disadvantaged schools directly addresses one documented network-level consequence (staffing dilution) of selective-track outflow
threats: Because outflow toward selective, geographically clustered institutions continues, compensatory funding may only partially offset the structural network losses in feeder schools rather than resolve them
weaknesses: Does not address the geographic concentration of selective tracks in urban/Budapest areas, nor the stagnant capacity constraint, leaving core network-access asymmetries fully intact

Continued high-performer and funding outflow from feeder schools toward geographically concentrated selective institutions persists unabated, since the underlying network structure generating that outflow is unchanged
